title: Deploying a Web Application with Nginx and SSL
description: Install Nginx on your Ubuntu server, configure server blocks for your domain, and set up free SSL certificates with Let's Encrypt and Certbot.
prerequisites: A secured Ubuntu 24.04 server (see exercise 2: Securing Your Linux Server)
A domain name pointed to your server's IP address
SSH access as a sudo user
UFW firewall configured with ports 80 and 443 open
learningObjectives: Install and configure Nginx as a web server
Set up server blocks (virtual hosts) for a domain
Deploy a static website to the server
Install Certbot and obtain a free SSL certificate from Let's Encrypt
Configure automatic HTTP to HTTPS redirection

sponsorCta.text: You need a running Linux server for this exercise. Any Ubuntu server works, but we recommend DigitalOcean for quick setup.

steps.title: Install Nginx
steps.description: Install Nginx from the Ubuntu package repository and verify it is running.
steps.expectedOutput:
● nginx.service - A high performance web server and a reverse proxy server
     Loaded: loaded (/lib/systemd/system/nginx.service; enabled; preset: enabled)
     Active: active (running) since ...
...

nginx version: nginx/1.24.0 (Ubuntu)
hints: Nginx starts automatically after installation on Ubuntu
If you visit your server's IP in a browser, you should see the default Nginx welcome page
If UFW is running, ports 80 and 443 should already be allowed from the security exercise
validationCriteria: Nginx is installed and running
systemctl status shows 'active (running)'
Visiting http://YOUR_SERVER_IP in a browser shows the Nginx welcome page

steps.title: Create the Website Directory and a Sample Page
steps.description: Set up the directory structure for your website and create a simple HTML page to test the configuration.
steps.expectedOutput:
No output on success. Verify with:
ls -la /var/www/yourdomain.com/html/
total 12
drwxr-xr-x 2 deploy deploy 4096 ... .
drwxr-xr-x 3 deploy deploy 4096 ... ..
-rw-r--r-- 1 deploy deploy  XXX ... index.html
hints: Replace 'yourdomain.com' with your actual domain name throughout this exercise
The $USER variable expands to your current username
You can create the index.html file with nano or vim: nano /var/www/yourdomain.com/html/index.html
validationCriteria: The directory /var/www/yourdomain.com/html exists
index.html is present in the html directory
The file is owned by your user, not root

steps.title: Create an Nginx Server Block
steps.description: Nginx uses server blocks (similar to Apache virtual hosts) to serve different sites. Create one for your domain.
steps.expectedOutput: The tee command prints the file contents back to your terminal as confirmation.
hints: Replace 'yourdomain.com' with your actual domain everywhere in the config
The try_files directive tells Nginx to serve the requested file, then try it as a directory, then return 404
Separate log files per site make debugging much easier
validationCriteria: The config file exists at /etc/nginx/sites-available/yourdomain.com
server_name matches your domain
root points to your website directory

steps.title: Enable the Server Block and Test
steps.description: Create a symlink to enable the site, test the Nginx configuration, and reload.
steps.expectedOutput:
nginx: the configuration file /etc/nginx/nginx.conf syntax is ok
nginx: configuration file /etc/nginx/nginx.conf test is successful
hints: Always run nginx -t before reloading to catch config errors
Reload is safer than restart because it applies changes without dropping connections
If nginx -t fails, check for typos in your server block file
Visit http://yourdomain.com in your browser to verify the site is working
validationCriteria: Symlink exists in /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/
nginx -t reports no errors
Your domain serves the custom index.html page

steps.title: Verify Your Domain Points to the Server
steps.description: Before requesting an SSL certificate, your domain must resolve to your server's IP address. Verify this with dig or nslookup.
steps.expectedOutput:
YOUR_SERVER_IP
YOUR_SERVER_IP

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Server: nginx/1.24.0 (Ubuntu)
Content-Type: text/html
...
hints: Set an A record for yourdomain.com and www.yourdomain.com in your DNS provider
DNS changes can take up to 48 hours to propagate, but usually take 5-15 minutes
If dig is not installed, run: sudo apt install -y dnsutils
Certbot will fail if the domain does not resolve to your server
validationCriteria: Both the bare domain and www subdomain resolve to your server's IP
curl returns a 200 status from your Nginx server

steps.title: Install Certbot and Obtain an SSL Certificate
steps.description: Certbot is the official Let's Encrypt client. It handles obtaining and installing SSL certificates automatically.
steps.expectedOutput:
Saving debug log to /var/log/letsencrypt/letsencrypt.log
Enter email address (used for urgent renewal and security notices):
...
Successfully received certificate.
Certificate is saved at: /etc/letsencrypt/live/yourdomain.com/fullchain.pem
Key is saved at:         /etc/letsencrypt/live/yourdomain.com/privkey.pem
...
Deploying certificate
Successfully deployed certificate for yourdomain.com
Successfully deployed certificate for www.yourdomain.com
...
Congratulations! You have successfully enabled HTTPS
hints: Certbot will ask for your email and to agree to the terms of service
The --nginx flag tells Certbot to automatically modify your Nginx config
Let's Encrypt certificates are free and valid for 90 days
If Certbot fails, check that your domain resolves to this server and ports 80/443 are open
validationCriteria: Certbot runs without errors
Certificate files exist in /etc/letsencrypt/live/yourdomain.com/
Visiting https://yourdomain.com works and shows a valid certificate

steps.title: Verify HTTPS and HTTP Redirect
steps.description: Certbot should have configured Nginx to redirect HTTP traffic to HTTPS automatically. Verify both the redirect and the SSL certificate.
steps.expectedOutput:
HTTP/1.1 301 Moved Permanently
Server: nginx/1.24.0 (Ubuntu)
Location: https://yourdomain.com/
...

HTTP/2 200
server: nginx/1.24.0 (Ubuntu)
content-type: text/html
strict-transport-security: max-age=31536000
...
hints: HTTP requests should return a 301 redirect to HTTPS
HTTPS requests should return 200 with your page content
Certbot adds SSL configuration lines and a redirect block to your Nginx config
Check the certificate in your browser by clicking the padlock icon
validationCriteria: HTTP requests redirect to HTTPS with a 301 status
HTTPS requests return 200 OK
The SSL certificate is valid and issued by Let's Encrypt
Nginx config test passes

steps.title: Set Up Automatic Certificate Renewal
steps.description: Let's Encrypt certificates expire after 90 days. Certbot installs a systemd timer to renew them automatically. Verify it is set up and test a dry run.
commands: sudo cat /etc/systemd/system/snap.certbot.renew.timer 2>/dev/null || sudo systemctl status certbot.timer
steps.expectedOutput:
... certbot.timer    certbot.service    ...

Saving debug log to /var/log/letsencrypt/letsencrypt.log

- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -
Processing /etc/letsencrypt/renewal/yourdomain.com.conf
- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -
Simulating renewal of an existing certificate...

Congratulations, all simulated renewals succeeded:
  /etc/letsencrypt/live/yourdomain.com/fullchain.pem (success)
hints: The dry run simulates renewal without actually requesting a new certificate
Certbot's timer runs twice daily and renews certificates that expire within 30 days
You do not need to set up a cron job; the systemd timer handles it
To manually renew: sudo certbot renew
validationCriteria: The certbot timer is listed and active
The dry run completes without errors
Automatic renewal is confirmed working

completionCriteria: Nginx is installed and serving your website
A server block is configured for your domain
SSL certificate is installed and HTTPS is working
Automatic certificate renewal is set up and tested

resources.title: How To Install Nginx on Ubuntu 24.04
Certbot Instructions for Nginx on Ubuntu

troubleshooting.issue: Certbot fails with 'Could not automatically find a matching server block'
troubleshooting.solution: Make sure the server_name in your Nginx config exactly matches the domain you pass to certbot with -d. Also verify the site is enabled (symlinked in sites-enabled) and that you ran nginx -t and reloaded.

troubleshooting.issue: Certbot fails with a connection timeout or 'urn:ietf:params:acme:error:connection'
troubleshooting.solution: Let's Encrypt needs to reach your server on port 80. Verify your DNS points to the correct IP, UFW allows port 80, and no other firewall or cloud security group is blocking traffic.

troubleshooting.issue: Browser shows 'Your connection is not private' after installing the certificate
troubleshooting.solution: Clear your browser cache or try an incognito window. If the issue persists, check 'sudo certbot certificates' to verify the certificate is valid and covers the correct domains.
